metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `necessity` gets a static YAML `criteria:` slot (new pattern) or routes through `ProductionBatchRunner` like R1/R3 (`asserted-capability`, `decomp-shape`) — if the latter, remove the `plan-review.yaml` criteria-list edit and confirm the ProductionBatchRunner path handles necessity without a YAML slot.
evidence: plan-review.yaml criteria list (lines 55-119): contains A1, COH, E1-E6, F1, F4, G1G2, G3-G7, T1-T14 — no slot for asserted-capability, decomp-shape, removal-rationale, hedge, T13, T14
Search for 'asserted-capability' and 'removal-rationale' in plan-review.yaml returns no matches
ProductionBatchRunner._resolve_criteria reads req.criteria (the already-when-gated set) and resolves by prompt-id — the R1/R3 criteria land there via this path, not a static YAML slot
Plan's own rationale: 'Reuse the R1/R3 advisory-criterion seam wholesale'
findings.finding: The plan states it will 'Wire [necessity] into the production Pass-1 finder batch (`workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` `criteria:` list, gated by `when: include_necessity`)' and claims this 'reuse[s] the R1/R3 advisory-criterion seam wholesale'. However, the prior-art R1 (`asserted-capability`) and R3 (`decomp-shape`) criteria — and every other recently-added advisory criterion (`removal-rationale`, `hedge`, `T13`, `T14`) — have NO static slot in `plan-review.yaml`'s `criteria:` list. They route through `ProductionBatchRunner` (which resolves them via `registry.by_id()` after `when`-gating). Adding a YAML `criteria:` slot for `necessity` diverges from the R1/R3 seam. It also creates an unenumerated consumer: every downstream system that reads the static `criteria:` list in the YAML (linters, schema validators, the engine's include-key resolution) would need to handle the new slot.
findings.location: What — piece (a) section: 'Wire it into the production Pass-1 finder batch (`workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` `criteria:` list, gated by `when: include_necessity`)'
scenarios: R1/R3 are not in the YAML so `plan_review_assemble_criteria` emits no `include_asserted-capability` / `include_decomp-shape` booleans — those criteria are fan-in via _project_criteria/route_criteria in ProductionBatchRunner. Adding a static YAML slot for `necessity` would make it route differently than R1/R3.

If necessity IS added to the YAML criteria list but `assemble_criteria` does not emit `include_necessity` for bugs (because bugs set `run_llm=True` but `applies()` suppresses necessity for bugs... but the plan says `suppress_types` is NOT set for necessity), the boolean would be emitted True for bug tickets, and the criteria-list slot would cause the finder to run necessity even in contexts where the bug-tier restriction in `assemble_criteria` might interfere.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `necessity` rubric file's internal protocol: name the two CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS (gate sub-answer + grounded sub-answer), their `{yes|no|insufficient}` vocabulary, the gate condition ('no → not-applicable → PASS'), the FIRE-ON rule (what makes a plan fail necessity), the PASS rule (what counts as justified motivation/reproduction), and the FAIL-OPEN/abstain clause for ambiguous cases — mirroring the explicit protocol in `plan_review_asserted_capability.md` lines 52-58.
evidence: Plan text: 'reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md: the rubric prompt (gate sub-answer + grounded sub-answer)'
ABSENCE: no vocabulary for the gate sub-answer question, no FIRE-ON condition, no FAIL-OPEN clause, no criterion-local CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS defined anywhere in the plan

Contrast: plan_review_asserted_capability.md lines 52-58: 'CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS (criterion-local): asserts_named_module_capability {yes|no|insufficient} — the GATE... asserted_capability_grounded {yes|no|insufficient} — only meaningful when gated in'

Contrast: plan_review_decomp_shape.md lines 60-66: 'has_container_decomposition {yes|no|insufficient} — the GATE: is the parent decomposed into >=2 children... decomposition_shape_sound {yes|no|insufficient}'
findings.finding: The `necessity` rubric file (`reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`) is named but its internal structure — specifically the CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS (the gate sub-answer and grounded sub-answer, their vocabulary, and the gate logic that determines when each applies) — is never defined in the plan. The R1/R3 analog files (`plan_review_asserted_capability.md`, `plan_review_decomp_shape.md`) each specify two named sub-answers with explicit `{yes|no|insufficient}` vocabulary and gate semantics ('no → not-applicable → PASS'). The plan says only 'the rubric prompt (gate sub-answer + grounded sub-answer)' — naming the pattern but providing no vocabulary, no gate condition, no FIRE-ON/PASS-ON rules, and no FAIL-OPEN (abstain-with-coverage) clause for necessity. An executor writing this rubric file has no specification to work from.
findings.location: What — piece (a): necessity / no-op probe; Scope section: `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`
scenarios: An executor writing plan_review_necessity.md has no spec for what question to ask (the gate sub-answer), what vocabulary to use ({yes|no/insufficient}?), what FIRE-ON conditions trigger a finding, or what constitutes a justified no-op / docs-only / well-motivated outcome that passes — the plan describes the criterion's purpose but not the rubric's internal protocol.

The `rebar criteria eval necessity` sanity run (AC 3) exercises the rubric, but if the rubric is authored without a specified gate logic, the eval targets (precision/recall) cannot be systematically met.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-section to the plan's piece (a) description: 'CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS: `change_motivated {yes|no|insufficient}` — the GATE: does the plan state a concrete problem/symptom/measurement motivating the change (reproduction, metric, failure mode, or user impact)? `no` → not-applicable-style finding (necessity fires). `motivation_grounded {yes|no|insufficient}` — only when gated: is the motivation specific enough that an executor can verify the need is real (not just "we think this would be better")? Fire a finding when `change_motivated=no` OR `motivation_grounded=no`; abstain (`insufficient`) when the plan is ambiguous about whether a mechanism already exists.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, sufficient
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC-5) that verifies `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` contains a `necessity` section — e.g. `grep -q 'necessity' docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` or a doc-content assertion in the unit tests.
evidence: Plan text: 'reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md: the rubric prompt (gate sub-answer + grounded sub-answer), and regenerate `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md`.'
Scope section lists 'docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md (regenerated)' as an explicit output.
ABSENCE: None of the six AC lines contain an assertion or artifact check for docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md content or existence.
findings.finding: The `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` regeneration is listed as a deliverable in piece (a) and the Scope section but has no covering acceptance criterion — no AC verifies that the guide was actually regenerated with the necessity section.
findings.location: What — piece (a) ('regenerate `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md`'); Scope section; Acceptance Criteria (all 6 AC lines)
scenarios: An implementer ships the necessity rubric prompt but forgets to regenerate the guide; the gap is invisible because no AC catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC-5 to: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md documents the bug review tier, the necessity probe, and the promotion gate (d8a5); AND docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md contains a necessity criterion entry (verified by grep or unit test).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `applies_at.scope:["leaf"]` routing constraint on necessity with the bug-tier's unconditional claim: either (a) document that bugs are always leaves in this system (satisfying the constraint trivially), or (b) add `"bug"` to a `force_scope_override` mechanism so necessity always fires for bugs regardless of scope, or (c) add a unit test asserting a non-leaf bug still receives an advisory necessity result.
evidence: Piece (a): 'applies_at.scope:["leaf"]'
Piece (b): 'for ticket_type == "bug", run the DET floor … set run_llm:True' — no scope qualification.
ABSENCE: The plan does not assert that all bug tickets are guaranteed to be leaf scope, nor does it specify what the bug tier should do when a bug is non-leaf.
findings.finding: The `applies_at.scope:["leaf"]` routing constraint on necessity (piece (a)) and the piece (b) claim that bugs get the DET floor + necessity are in tension for non-leaf bug tickets: a bug scoped as a non-leaf would satisfy the bug-tier precheck (run_llm=True, necessity included) but the routing engine's scope filter would silently skip necessity, producing an empty LLM result rather than the advisory review piece (b) promises.
findings.location: What — piece (a) (applies_at.scope:["leaf"] config); What — piece (b) ('for ticket_type=="bug", restrict included LLM criteria to BUG_TIER_CRITERIA=("necessity",)')
scenarios: A non-leaf bug (e.g., a bug used as a parent in an unusual workflow) reaches the gate; precheck emits run_llm=True and includes necessity, but the routing scope filter drops it; the result is an advisory tier with zero LLM criteria evaluated — behaviorally closer to exempt-PASS than to the 'light advisory review' promised by piece (b).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequence) for the bug-tier behavioral change: document how to revert bugs to exempt-PASS if the advisory tier proves noisy, and identify any downstream consumers of the `runner:"exempt"` REVIEW_RESULT shape that need migration or a compat shim.
evidence: Plan explicitly notes bug 5886's persisted REVIEW_RESULT was `{"runner":"exempt","verdict":"PASS","llm_calls":0}` — a concrete prior contract
Plan says 'Bugs stop short-circuiting to bare exempt-PASS' with no mention of rollback or feature flag
The standing recorder (d8a5) reads REVIEW_RESULT and would see a schema change for all bug tickets
No expand-contract sequence or back-out described anywhere in Scope or Approach sections
findings.finding: The plan changes the REVIEW_RESULT shape for all bug tickets (from `{"runner":"exempt","verdict":"PASS","llm_calls":0}` to a full light-tier result) but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequence if the bug tier proves noisy or if downstream consumers (the standing recorder, CI tooling, persisted-result parsers) rely on the `runner:"exempt"` shape for bugs.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck
scenarios: The standing recorder or a monitoring dashboard that checks `runner=="exempt"` for bugs silently changes behavior after deploy
Advisory noise from the necessity probe on well-motivated bugs prompts a desire to revert, but no mechanism exists
A rollback of the change requires manual code surgery with no documented procedure
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `rollback:` section to the plan noting: (1) the `when: include_necessity` gate flag in `plan-review.yaml` can be set to exclude `necessity` to revert the probe; (2) the bug-tier precheck change can be reverted by re-enabling the short-circuit; (3) confirm the standing recorder and any CI scripts that parse REVIEW_RESULT either tolerate the new shape or are updated in the same change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior for LLM call failure in the bug review tier (e.g., degrade to exempt-PASS with a logged warning), and add an AC or unit-test assertion that a mocked LLM exception in the necessity probe does not produce a blocking verdict or unhandled exception for a bug ticket.
evidence: Plan says 'run the DET floor, DOWNGRADE all DET findings to advisory, set run_llm:True' but names no error path
'never blocking a bug' is described as structural (advisory posture + empty det_blocking) but this invariant relies on the LLM call completing without exception
No retry/backoff/fallback for the new bug-tier LLM call is mentioned anywhere in the plan
The pre-existing exempt path produced llm_calls:0, so no existing LLM error-handling for bugs existed to inherit
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new LLM call for every bug ticket review (necessity probe) but does not specify the fallback/error behavior when that LLM call fails — e.g., network error, timeout, or provider outage. The 'never block a bug' invariant must hold even under LLM failure, but the plan is silent on whether the tier degrades to exempt-PASS, surfaces a warning, or propagates an exception that could surface as a confusing error.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck spec
scenarios: LLM provider is unavailable during a bug review run — the necessity probe call raises; without a specified fallback the tier could propagate an unhandled exception
Transient timeout on the necessity probe call causes the entire gate run to fail, blocking CI unexpectedly — violating the 'never block a bug' invariant through an indirect path
Partial LLM response (truncated output) produces a malformed criterion result with no specified recovery
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck spec, add: 'If the necessity LLM call raises or returns an unparseable result, downgrade to exempt-PASS with a warning logged and coverage.bug_tier stamped as degraded — the bug is never blocked by a probe failure.' Add a unit test in test_bug_review_tier.py that injects a mocked LLM exception and asserts verdict != BLOCKING and no exception propagates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what controls `when: include_necessity` in `plan-review.yaml` (e.g., a top-level config key, a YAML boolean defaulting to `true`, or a feature-flag entry) so an executor can implement it without guessing.
evidence: "Wire it into the production Pass-1 finder batch (`workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` `criteria:` list, gated by `when: include_necessity`) so it actually runs on leaf plan reviews."
No definition of `include_necessity` appears anywhere in the plan — not in Scope, Approach, or ACs.
findings.finding: The `when: include_necessity` condition on the `plan-review.yaml` finder batch slot is not defined. The plan says to wire `necessity` into the production Pass-1 finder batch 'gated by `when: include_necessity`' but does not specify what controls this condition — whether it is a YAML boolean, a config key, a feature-flag entry, or a separate settings file. An executor cannot implement this gate slot without knowing what `include_necessity` evaluates to or where it is declared.
findings.location: What — piece (a): `workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` `criteria:` list, `when: include_necessity`
scenarios: Executor adds the YAML entry but cannot resolve what `when: include_necessity` maps to, forcing a design decision at implementation time
The criterion silently never runs in production if the condition is mis-declared
findings.suggested_fix: State the default explicitly: e.g., 'Add `include_necessity: true` as a top-level key in `plan-review.yaml` (or the gate config layer that resolves `when:` conditions); the condition defaults to `true` so necessity runs on all leaf reviews from day one, and operators can set it to `false` to temporarily suppress it during rollout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interaction rule between bug-tier restriction (`BUG_TIER_CRITERIA`) and PROBE MODE (`probe_criteria`) in `plan_review_assemble_criteria`: e.g., 'probe_criteria takes precedence over bug-tier restriction when active (the probe is a diagnostic tool, not subject to ticket-type gating)' or 'bug-tier AND probe are intersected'.
evidence: Plan text: 'workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria: for ticket_type == "bug", restrict the included LLM criteria to registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA (= ("necessity",))'
workflow_ops.py lines 248-269: existing PROBE MODE: 'probe = {str(c) for c in (ctx.inputs.get("probe_criteria") or [])}; if probe: included = {cid for cid in effective if cid in probe}'
ABSENCE: no specification of how bug-tier restriction and probe_criteria interact in the plan
findings.finding: The protocol for how `bug_tier` restriction in `plan_review_assemble_criteria` interacts with the existing PROBE MODE (`probe_criteria` bypass) is not specified. The existing `plan_review_assemble_criteria` has a PROBE MODE path (`probe = {str(c) for c in (ctx.inputs.get('probe_criteria') or [])}`) that, when active, bypasses `applies()/overlay routing` entirely and forces exactly the probe allowlist. The plan says bugs should 'restrict the included LLM criteria to `registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA`' via `plan_review_assemble_criteria`, but never specifies whether: (a) the bug-tier restriction replaces the probe_criteria bypass, (b) they compose (probe_criteria ∩ BUG_TIER_CRITERIA), or (c) bug-tier takes precedence. This is a decision rule that is referenced but not co-located with the schema (the assemble step's output) that consumes it.
findings.location: What — piece (b): `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria` — bug-tier restriction
scenarios: A drift-refresh probe (`probe_criteria=["E4","G1G2"]`) is run against a bug ticket. Without a specified interaction rule, the executor must decide whether the bug gets E4+G1G2 (probe wins), necessity only (bug-tier wins), or {} (intersection is empty). The wrong choice either silently skips the probe or silently skips the bug-tier restriction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the value of `coverage.bug_tier` in the plan (e.g., `True` / `{"det_downgraded": True}` / `"light_advisory"`) so the unit test can assert a specific value and downstream consumers have a stable contract.
evidence: Plan text: 'set `run_llm:True`, and stamp `coverage.bug_tier`'
AC text: 'precheck emits `run_llm=True` + `det_blocking==[]` + `coverage.bug_tier` for a bug'
ABSENCE: no specification of what value `coverage.bug_tier` holds — boolean, string, dict, or just key presence

Contrast: existing coverage keys in workflow_ops.py lines 528-530: 'coverage = {"det": ctx.inputs.get("det_coverage") or {}, "routing": ctx.inputs.get("routing") or {}, "llm_ran": True}' — each existing key has a defined value type
findings.finding: The `coverage.bug_tier` stamp referenced in the plan for the bug-tier precheck path has no value vocabulary defined. The plan says 'stamp `coverage.bug_tier`' but does not specify what value this key takes (a boolean `True`, a string like `"light_advisory"`, a dict `{"tier": "bug", "det_downgraded": true}`, or simply the key's presence). The existing `det_coverage` output (used in `plan_review_precheck_output`) is a `dict[str, Any]`. The unit-test AC explicitly pins 'precheck emits `run_llm=True` + `det_blocking==[]` + `coverage.bug_tier` for a bug' — but without a defined value, the unit test cannot assert a specific value and cannot catch a wrong implementation.
findings.location: What — piece (b): `workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck` — `coverage.bug_tier` stamp; AC line: 'precheck emits `run_llm=True` + `det_blocking==[]` + `coverage.bug_tier` for a bug'
scenarios: The unit test for `coverage.bug_tier` asserts `output['det_coverage']['bug_tier']` exists but not its value — the test passes for any value including `None`, failing to pin the contract.
Downstream consumers (e.g., the standing recorder, effectiveness harness) that read `coverage.bug_tier` to identify bug-tier runs cannot parse the value without a defined schema.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `coverage.bug_tier = True` (a boolean presence-stamp, consistent with the simplest pattern) and have the unit test assert `output['det_coverage'].get('bug_tier') is True`.

findings.finding: The plan names the rubric prompt file as `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` under `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` (i.e., `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`), but that directory does not exist. All existing plan-review rubric prompts live at `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md` — confirmed by the `reviewers/` listing (plan_review_A1.md, plan_review_asserted_capability.md, plan_review_decomp_shape.md, etc.) and by the registry.py docstring: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md`.
findings.location: What — piece (a) / Scope section: `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Place the new rubric at `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` — matching the existing pattern for every other plan-review criterion rubric (plan_review_A1.md, plan_review_asserted_capability.md, etc.).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all consumers of `REVIEW_RESULT` that could key on `runner='exempt'` or `llm_calls=0` for bug tickets, and confirm each is compatible with (or updated for) the new non-exempt bug-tier result shape.
evidence: Plan states: 'Bugs stop short-circuiting to bare exempt-PASS; instead the plan-review gate runs a LIGHT ADVISORY tier'
Plan verifies: 'verdict runner != "exempt"' in bug_tier_sanity.json AC
Plan acknowledges only: 'The CLI claim-time bug exemption (_commands/gates.py:93) is UNCHANGED'
Bug 5886 persisted result was {"runner":"exempt","verdict":"PASS","llm_calls":0} — any consumer pattern-matching these fields for bugs would break
findings.finding: The plan changes `workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck` so bugs no longer produce `runner='exempt'`/`llm_calls=0`, but does not enumerate other consumers of `REVIEW_RESULT` (dashboard, metrics, downstream scripts, CI reporters) that may key on `runner == 'exempt'` or `llm_calls == 0` for bug tickets. The only acknowledged caller is `_commands/gates.py:93` (claim-time exemption, unchanged).
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / 'workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck' bullet; AC bullet 3 (bug_tier_sanity.json)
scenarios: A CI reporter or metrics dashboard that treats runner='exempt' as 'no review needed' for bugs now sees an unexpected non-exempt result and misbehaves or raises a false alert.
A downstream script that checks llm_calls==0 to skip further processing for bugs now runs additional processing on every bug plan review.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Caller impact' subsection to piece (b) listing known REVIEW_RESULT consumers (dashboard, metrics, CI reporters, downstream automation) and stating whether each is unaffected, updated, or explicitly out-of-scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rubric file path in Scope to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` (not `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`), matching the `plan_review_<id>.md` convention documented in registry.py and used by all existing criteria including R1/R3.
evidence: list_directory('src/rebar/llm/plan_review') shows no `reviewers/` subdirectory
list_directory('src/rebar/llm/reviewers') shows plan_review_asserted_capability.md, plan_review_decomp_shape.md, plan_review_removal_rationale.md etc.
registry.py line 6: 'src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md'
test_asserted_capability_criterion.py line 69: reads from 'src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_asserted_capability.md'
findings.finding: The plan names the rubric file as `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` under `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` in its Scope section, but that directory does not exist (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/` is absent). All existing plan-review rubric files live at `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_*.md` (e.g. `plan_review_asserted_capability.md`, `plan_review_decomp_shape.md`). The registry docstring explicitly documents `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md` as the prompt-library location. The unit test for R1 reads from `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_asserted_capability.md`. Naming the wrong path risks misplacing the rubric file and breaking `check_registry_coverage`.
findings.location: Scope section: `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`
scenarios: Implementer places the rubric at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` (creating a new dir); `get_prompt('plan-review-necessity')` resolves via the da27 prompt machinery to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` instead, causing RegistryError/FileNotFoundError at load_criteria time and failing check_registry_coverage.
`validate_packaged_routing()` passes (routing entry is present) but `check_registry_coverage()` fails because the prompt file is at the wrong path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet, change `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` (the `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` prompt library, not a subdirectory of `plan_review/`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) update parent epic 6982 first to authorize the gate substitution (replacing 'retrospective gate on E2 class-4 corpus' with 'bounded hand-authored fixture sanity eval') and re-review the parent, or (b) align R4 with the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE by closing R4 deferred until E2 delivers class-4 mutants — do not unilaterally redefine the gate type in the leaf without the parent's authorization.
evidence: Parent 6982 Context explicitly labels R4 as a 'retrospective gate': 'R4=03a9 (necessity probe + bug tier, retrospective gate)'
Parent 6982 AC: 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate'

Parent 6982 INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE: 'an experiment that fails its own validity gate or returns an ambiguous result gets at most ONE documented re-run with changed method; if still not clearly passing, every dependent R-story closes deferred with the results recorded — ambiguity never rounds up to a pass'

R4 plan: 'this re-plan DROPS the E2 dependency (depends_on d125 removed) and validates with BOUNDED, HAND-AUTHORED fixtures run through rebar criteria eval (<=~8 live single-criterion runs, NOT a batch)' — a gate-type substitution, not a deferral

R4's own comment (filed before this re-plan) reached the INCONCLUSIVE-GATE conclusion: 'R4 must not land until E2 delivers class-4 mutants' and 'RECOMMENDATION: (1) complete E2 (d125) to commit the class-4 mutant corpus... then (3) resume R4'
findings.finding: R4 unilaterally drops the 'retrospective gate' (E2 class-4 mutant corpus) the parent commits to and substitutes a different gate type (hand-authored bounded fixtures via `rebar criteria eval`) without first updating the parent. The parent's governance model channels an unrunnable gate into deferral (INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE), not gate-type substitution; R4's plan skips the deferral path and redefines its own eval gate, stepping outside the parent's authorized scope for this story.
findings.location: R4 'Advisory-first strategy' section ('this re-plan DROPS the E2 dependency ... and validates with BOUNDED, HAND-AUTHORED fixtures') vs. parent 6982 Context ('R4=03a9 (necessity probe + bug tier, retrospective gate)') and parent AC ('no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate') and parent INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE
scenarios: An implementer lands R4 with hand-authored fixtures as the gate — the parent's requirement that every R-story land only after its eval gate passes is technically met by the leaf's own AC (bounded fixtures pass), but the parent's retrospective-gate intent (discrimination on a seeded-defect corpus) is unverified.

The parent epic's AC audit finds R4 landed with a substituted gate type not authorized by the parent, triggering a compliance question about whether the parent's overall gate-governance integrity holds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Gate substitution rationale' sub-section to R4's plan that explicitly invokes the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE language, explains why the bounded fixture approach satisfies the parent's 'no detection-bearing recommendation without its eval gate' intent (e.g., the necessity probe is advisory-only and ships BEFORE its retrospective gate, analogous to how R6 ships first with no eval gate), and records an operator decision authorizing the substitution. Then update the parent epic to reflect the changed gate type for R4. Without this, the parent and leaf are in conflict on the gate-governance path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) with a concrete proving command that verifies `necessity` appears in `plan-review.yaml`'s `criteria:` list, e.g.: `python -c "import yaml; d=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml')); assert 'necessity' in d['criteria']"` exits 0.
evidence: AC1 checks `criteria_routing.json` and `registry.py` only
AC2 checks unit tests for routing/prompt-contract/fixture-shape
AC3 exercises `rebar criteria eval` (CLI, not the gate YAML path) and the bug_tier lifecycle
No AC asserts that `plan-review.yaml`'s `criteria:` list contains `'necessity'`
The YAML file is listed in Scope as a deliverable but has no corresponding proving command in the ACs
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion validates that `plan-review.yaml` was actually updated to include `necessity` in its `criteria:` list. All five non-attested ACs (registry assertion, pytest, criteria eval, effectiveness script, docs) can be satisfied without ever modifying the YAML gate file, leaving the production wiring gap undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — union-of-steps reachability; Scope: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`
scenarios: The YAML file is never updated; all ACs still pass; `necessity` silently never runs in the production gate
A reviewer signs off on a green AC run that never validated the production wiring
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ACs: `python -c "import yaml; cfg=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml')); assert 'necessity' in [e if isinstance(e,str) else e.get('name') for e in cfg['criteria']]"` exits 0 — this proves the YAML wiring is in place.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what controls `when: include_necessity` in `plan-review.yaml` (default value, who sets it, whether it is always-true post-ship or a feature-flag), and add an AC that verifies necessity actually fires on a real or integration-level plan review after wiring (e.g., a grep/assertion that the criteria list contains 'necessity' when the flag's condition is met).
evidence: 'gated by `when: include_necessity`' appears once with no definition of the flag or its default
No AC asserts that the `when: include_necessity` condition is true in production after ship
Unit tests and live sanity cover necessity's logic and LLM behavior but not the YAML gate condition that controls whether it fires in practice
findings.finding: The plan wires `necessity` into the production Pass-1 finder batch 'gated by `when: include_necessity`' but never specifies what controls this flag, what its default value is, or when it evaluates to true in production. No AC verifies that `include_necessity` is satisfied post-ship, so the criterion could be silently dormant despite being registered and unit-tested.
findings.location: What — piece (a): necessity / no-op probe; last bullet: 'Wire it into the production Pass-1 finder batch (workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml criteria: list, gated by when: include_necessity)'
scenarios: `include_necessity` defaults to false or is never set in the production environment; necessity is registered, passes all tests, and has green eval artifacts, but never fires on any real plan review.
A future change to plan-review.yaml omits or misspells the flag; necessity silently stops running with no test catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: If `include_necessity` is always-true post-ship (the expected case given this is an advisory criterion), replace the conditional with an unconditional entry and drop the flag. If it is a feature flag, document the flag name, its default, and add an AC: `python -c "import yaml; c=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml')); assert 'necessity' in [e['criterion'] for e in c['criteria']]"` exits 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA` has ≥3 real call-sites before exporting it as a named constant; if only `plan_review_assemble_criteria` consumes it, use an inline `("necessity",)` literal and drop the named export (promotes it when a third consumer exists).
evidence: Grep confirms `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA` does not yet exist in the codebase.

Plan identifies exactly two consumers: `plan_review_assemble_criteria` restricts a bug's included set, and the AC assertion (`assert r.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA==("necessity",)`). That is 2 call-sites, not ≥3.

A bare inline `("necessity",)` in `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria` (the single real consumer) and a direct tuple comparison in the AC would be simpler and equally maintainable until a third consumer emerges.
Rule-of-Three from A1 checklist: 'A proposed abstraction has >=3 existing call-sites or is premature (cite grep hits).'
findings.finding: `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA = ("necessity",)` is a single-element tuple constant in `registry.py` with only two identified call-sites (the `plan_review_assemble_criteria` op + the AC assertion command), falling below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a named abstraction.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / `registry.py`: add `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA = ("necessity",)`
scenarios: When the bug tier gains a second criterion later, the name `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA` earns its place — but that growth is hypothetical at ship time.
A future reader must look up the constant to discover it equals `("necessity",)`, which is less obvious than reading the literal in context.
findings.suggested_fix: In `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria`, inline `_BUG_ONLY_CRITERIA = {"necessity"}` as a module-private constant or a bare literal, and remove `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA` from `registry.py`'s public surface. Add `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA` to `registry.py` only when a second genuine call-site (outside the AC) requires it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that DET blocking findings for bug tickets are MOVED to det_advisory (not silently dropped), so the P1/P8 signal is preserved as advisory rather than lost when det_blocking is forced empty.
evidence: Plan says: 'run the DET floor, DOWNGRADE all DET findings to advisory (det_blocking:[] — a bug is never blocked), set run_llm:True'

Existing precheck code (workflow_ops.py:162-170): det_results = det_floor.run_det_floor(pctx); det_blocks = det_floor.det_blocking_findings(det_results) — these are real DetResult objects with blocking=True for P1/P8
P1 blocks when no ## Acceptance Criteria section exists (det_floor.py:163-191) — bug tickets routinely lack AC sections
The plan does not specify whether blocking DET results are moved to det_advisory or simply suppressed
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'DOWNGRADE all DET findings to advisory (det_blocking:[] — a bug is never blocked)' but does not specify where the actual DET blocking DetResults (P1, P8) go when they fire on a bug ticket. The existing precheck returns P1 as a real blocking finding when the AC section is absent — common for bug tickets. Setting det_blocking=[] while leaving DET blocking results unrouted either silently discards them (losing real signal) or leaves the mechanism underspecified, depending on the implementation.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier, workflow_ops bullet: 'DOWNGRADE all DET findings to advisory (det_blocking:[] — a bug is never blocked)'
scenarios: A bug ticket with no '## Acceptance Criteria' section: P1 fires as a blocking DetResult; if det_blocking=[] and the P1 finding is not moved to det_advisory, the signal is silently lost
A very large bug ticket: P8 fires as a blocking DetResult; same loss scenario
findings.suggested_fix: In the precheck spec for the bug tier, replace 'set det_blocking:[]' with 'move det_blocking results to det_advisory (downgrade blocking→advisory, never discard); det_blocking stays empty for the decide step.' This preserves the signal while enforcing the never-block invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the bug-tier BUG_TIER_CRITERIA restriction bypasses the applies() scope filter (mirroring probe_criteria mode), or alternatively confirm that necessity's scope should be ["leaf", "container"] when applied via the bug tier.
evidence: Plan specifies necessity: 'applies_at.scope:["leaf"]'
Plan specifies assemble_criteria: 'restrict the included LLM criteria to registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA (= ("necessity",))'
registry.applies() (registry.py:344-377): scope check — if scope=["leaf"] and ticket is a container (has_children=True), applies() returns False
probe_criteria mode (workflow_ops.py:253-256) bypasses applies() entirely: 'included = {cid for cid in effective if cid in probe}' — no applies() call
The plan never states whether BUG_TIER_CRITERIA restriction uses the probe-style bypass or goes through applies()
findings.finding: The plan specifies that necessity has applies_at.scope:["leaf"] but also says 'assemble restricts a bug's included set to BUG_TIER_CRITERIA=("necessity",)'. The existing plan_review_assemble_criteria op applies the applies() filter (which respects scope) unless probe_criteria mode bypasses it. The plan does not specify whether the bug-tier BUG_TIER_CRITERIA restriction bypasses the applies() filter — if it doesn't, necessity will be silently excluded on any container-bug (a bug ticket with children), producing a hollow bug-tier review identical to the old exempt-PASS.
findings.location: What — piece (b): workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria bullet; What — piece (a): criteria_routing.json entry applies_at.scope:["leaf"]
scenarios: A bug ticket that has sub-tasks (children): has_children=True, so scope=["leaf"] suppresses necessity via applies(), leaving the bug tier running zero LLM criteria — indistinguishable from the old exempt-PASS
findings.suggested_fix: Use the same bypass pattern as probe_criteria in assemble_criteria: when ticket_type=="bug", force included={cid for cid in effective if cid in BUG_TIER_CRITERIA} without calling applies(), ensuring necessity always runs regardless of container/leaf status.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and cite) that `criterion_effectiveness.py` discovers criterion ids dynamically from REVIEW_RESULT citations rather than a static allowlist, so that adding `necessity` to `CANONICAL_LLM` is sufficient for 'zero wiring' auto-inclusion; if a static list exists, add a scope note for the wiring step.
evidence: Plan states: 'Both criteria auto-flow into the standing recorder with zero wiring'

AC bullet 4 asserts the harness includes necessity 'when a REVIEW_RESULT cites it' — implying dynamic citation-scanning — but the harness implementation at docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/ is not inspected by the plan
ABSENCE: no explanation of how the harness discovers criterion ids — the mechanism is load-bearing for the 'zero wiring' claim
findings.finding: The plan asserts the standing recorder (criterion_effectiveness.py) auto-includes 'necessity' with 'zero wiring' — a capability claim about the harness's current implementation — but does not ground this against the harness source or explain the mechanism (e.g., whether it scans REVIEW_RESULT citation sets dynamically vs. a static criterion list). If the harness uses a static allowlist rather than dynamically scanning cited criteria, adding 'necessity' to CANONICAL_LLM is insufficient and the AC bullet 4 assertion fails silently.
findings.location: AC bullet 4: 'python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/criterion_effectiveness.py --report includes necessity when a REVIEW_RESULT cites it (pinned by the effectiveness unit test)'
scenarios: If criterion_effectiveness.py filters by a static criterion allowlist (not REVIEW_RESULT citation scanning), the 'zero wiring' property does not hold and necessity will be silently excluded from effectiveness reports until an explicit wiring step is added.
The unit test that 'pins' this may itself only assert the static interface, not the dynamic auto-inclusion behavior — so a green unit test would not catch the gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `necessity` checklist key names and check strings for `criteria_routing.json` in the plan (e.g., `change_motivated` / `motivation_grounded`), consistent with the sub-answer vocabulary specified in the rubric file.
evidence: Plan text: 'criteria_routing.json: new `necessity` entry — `exec:"1-TURN"`, `facet:"scope-intent"`, `default_posture:"advisory"`, `block_threshold:0.95`, `applies_at.scope:["leaf"]`, and deliberately NO `suppress_types`'
ABSENCE: no `checklist` key names defined for the necessity routing entry in the plan
Contrast: criteria_routing.json lines 12-29 (F1 entry): '"checklist": [{"key": "observable_outcome", "check": "..."}, ...]' — every routing entry carries a named checklist
registry.py line 462-484: validate_packaged_routing() checks every entry via _validate_routing_entry — malformed/missing checklist would fail CI
findings.finding: The `necessity` checklist key names for `criteria_routing.json` are not defined anywhere in the plan. Every other criterion's routing entry carries a `checklist` array with explicit, named keys (e.g., `F1`: `observable_outcome`, `in_session_evaluable`, etc.). The plan specifies all scalar routing fields for `necessity` (exec, facet, default_posture, block_threshold, applies_at) but omits the `checklist` array entirely. The `validate_packaged_routing` / `_validate_routing_entry` CI gate validates routing entry structure; if the checklist is absent or malformed, it will fail CI — but the executor has no specification of what keys to include.
findings.location: What — piece (a): `criteria_routing.json` entry specification
scenarios: The executor adds a necessity routing entry without a checklist (or with placeholder keys) — the CI gate either fails (if checklist is required) or passes with an underpopulated entry that misrepresents what the criterion checks.
The AC's Python assertion (`assert d['necessity']['default_posture']=='advisory'`) does not check the checklist keys, so this gap is not caught by the AC command.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
